top_weaknesses.issue: Lede is generic SaaS-history framing ('Software pricing used to hide behind access') — does not name AI agents or outcome pricing in the first 155 chars/first thumb-scroll
top_weaknesses.fix: Open with the AI-agent break or the Intercom Fin example; push the SaaS-history paragraph to second slot

top_weaknesses.issue: Voice repeatedly flips into LinkedIn-thought-leadership maxims ('They will win by naming the outcome, pricing it, and proving it happened', 'repeatable labor market wedge with software margins')
top_weaknesses.fix: Replace generalized closers with specific failure modes or numbers; cut three-part-symmetry summary lines

top_weaknesses.issue: Could use one compact list to enumerate outcome-pricing definitions (attribution, completion, escalation, refund, quality)
top_weaknesses.fix: Convert the sales/legal/support agent paragraph into a short bulleted list for skimmability

top_weaknesses.issue: Underuses search-intent phrases (usage-based pricing, value-based pricing, AI agent pricing)
top_weaknesses.fix: Naturally include those phrases in body without stuffing

consensus_summary: Strong, coherent argument with a clean H2 outline and a real thesis about pricing as architecture. Loses share-vector readers because the opening is abstract SaaS history and the closers slide into VC/LinkedIn-maxim voice.
